Ai ajustat beneficii, ai investit în traininguri, ai căutat soluții și totuși ceva nu s-a schimbat… Articolul Cum arată un leadership care scade rata de demisii și crește performanța sustenabilă. apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

medical.title: Teen leaders take on child marriage in Bangladesh
medical.summary: Child marriage is a persistent challenge in Bangladesh, but a project to tackle the practice has shown promise The post Teen leaders take on child marriage in Bangladesh appeared first on Positive News .

medical.title: Feeling more creative in nature? It might just be a feeling
medical.summary: Dr. Amy Lim, from Murdoch University's School of Psychology, and her team examined the effects of nature and non-natural stimuli on creative performance and intentions. The study, titled "Nature versus non-natural environments: Effects on stress and creativity," was published in The Journal of Environmental Psychology.

medical.title: As demand for GLP-1 pills and shots surges, healthy habits are still key
medical.summary: Whether they're using weekly shots or daily pills, more Americans than ever are turning to anti-obesity drugs to lose weight and boost health.

medical.title: UK study finds no added benefit of surfactant treatment for babies with severe bronchiolitis
medical.summary: A major UK-led clinical trial has found that a treatment commonly used to help premature babies breathe offers no benefit for infants on life support with severe bronchiolitis—a seasonal viral illness that hospitalizes thousands of babies each year. The Bronchiolitis Endotracheal Surfactant Study (BESS) trial is the largest-ever randomized study of surfactant for bronchiolitis.

medical.title: Children's extended social media use linked to increased depression and anxiety
medical.summary: Children who use social media for more than three hours per day are more likely to develop greater levels of depression and anxiety compared to those who use it more moderately. The findings are the latest analysis to come from the SCAMP study, led by public health researchers at Imperial College London and based on data from more than 2300 children in schools across London.

medical.title: Lyme disease vaccine shows over 70% efficacy, but phase three misses main goal
medical.summary: An experimental vaccine for Lyme disease is broadly effective, pharmaceutical companies Pfizer and Valneva announced Monday; however, the latter firm's shares fell because the clinical trial did not reach its main goal.

medical.title: FDA approves higher-dose Wegovy to help people lose more weight
medical.summary: A stronger version of the popular weight loss drug Wegovy is on the way after federal regulators signed off on a higher dose. The U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved a 7.2-milligram dose of Novo Nordisk's semaglutide, the active ingredient in Wegovy. Until now, the highest approved dose was 2.4 milligrams, taken as a weekly shot.

medical.title: Fathers' risk for psychiatric disorders increases a year after child's birth, study finds
medical.summary: Fathers in Sweden are less likely to receive a psychiatric diagnosis during their partner's pregnancy and in the months following the birth of their child. However, diagnoses of depression and stress-related disorders increase a year later, according to a new study published in JAMA Network Open by researchers at Karolinska Institutet in Sweden and Sichuan University in China.

medical.title: The prisoners flipping the script on stage
medical.summary: A theatre project brings prisoners to the stage, challenging how society sees them – and how they see themselves The post The prisoners flipping the script on stage appeared first on Positive News .

medical.title: Study finds two types of colon polyps can raise bowel cancer risk fivefold
medical.summary: Researchers studying over 8,400 colonoscopies discovered that having both adenomas and serrated polyps in the bowel can raise the risk of serious precancerous changes by up to five times. These two polyp types may represent separate cancer pathways that can occur at the same time. Nearly half of patients with serrated polyps also had adenomas, making this high-risk combination more common than expected. The results emphasize the importance of early detection and regular colonoscopy monitoring.

medical.title: Severe COVID or flu may raise lung cancer risk years later
medical.summary: A severe case of COVID-19 or influenza could increase the risk of lung cancer later on, according to new research. Scientists discovered that serious viral infections can alter immune cells in the lungs, leaving behind chronic inflammation that may help tumors develop months or years later. The increased risk was seen mainly after severe infections that required hospitalization. Vaccination, however, appears to prevent the dangerous lung changes.

medical.title: Monty Python Got It Wrong About Medieval Disease
medical.summary: In medieval Denmark, people could pay for more prestigious graves closer to the church — a sign of wealth and status. But when researchers examined hundreds of skeletons, they discovered something unexpected: even people with stigmatized diseases like leprosy were buried in these high-status spots. Instead of excluding the sick, many communities appear to have treated them much like everyone else.

science.title: Study finds overconfident CEOs are 10-15% less likely to delegate deal work
science.summary: A new study finds overconfident CEOs are less likely to delegate responsibilities to underlings, particularly in settings that involve complex transactions—such as hammering out the details of high-stakes deals. The paper, "Leave it to Me: Overconfident CEOs' Lower Propensity to Delegate Acquisition Responsibility," is published in the Journal of Management Studies.

science.title: What's that critter? New tech guidelines can help ensure we get the right answer
science.summary: Have you ever been in the woods and wondered what bug you're hearing or which bird is singing? These days, new technologies are helping both scientists and the public identify what's trilling, scuttling, tweeting, rustling, or slithering around a given area.

science.title: Snow flies produce bursts of heat and proteins to avoid freezing, new study finds
science.summary: In a new study, Northwestern University scientists explored how snow flies—small, wingless insects that crawl across snow to find mates and lay eggs—survive in freezing cold temperatures. They discovered this snow-dwelling fly uses a surprising combination of strategies: it generates its own body heat like a mammal and produces antifreeze proteins like an Arctic fish.

science.title: Preparing for the next pandemic: Scientists discover a new class of influenza antivirals
science.summary: Researchers from Leiden University, University of York, University of Barcelona/IQTCUB/ICREA and The Francis Crick Institute report a new class of experimental compounds that powerfully block influenza viruses. The findings offer a potential new strategy for treating flu infections and preparing for future pandemics. The study, published in Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, describes a family of molecules known as "sugar aziridines." These compounds are inspired by the widely used flu drug Oseltamivir (Tamiflu), but are designed to act in a fundamentally different way.

science.title: CERN hails delicate test on transporting antimatter as a scientific success
science.summary: Scientists in Geneva took some antiprotons out for a spin—a very delicate one—in a truck, in a never-tried-before test drive that has been deemed a success.

science.title: AI could spot the next financial crisis—but there's a catch
science.summary: What if AI could predict the next financial meltdown? Sounds like a promising idea, yet as new research finds, the devil is in the details.

science.title: NASA Research Proposes Technology to Seek Earth-Like Exoplanets
science.summary: As NASA seeks to understand the mysteries of the universe, the agency is advancing technologies to locate and explore Earth-like planets far beyond our solar system. A key element of this research involves observing reflected light from exoplanets, which can reveal indicators of Earth-like features such as water and oxygen. However, detecting this faint reflected […]

science.title: NASA’s Water-Hunting Tool Will Help Scout Moon’s South Pole
science.summary: NASA is joining international partners to hunt for ice on the Moon in support of future human exploration. The agency is providing a water-detecting instrument, the Neutron Spectrometer System (NSS), to the Lunar Polar Exploration (LUPEX) mission led by JAXA (Japan Aerospace Exploration Agency) and ISRO (Indian Space Research Organisation).

environment.title: Humpback whale recovery is changing who fathers the calves
environment.summary: A new study shows that as humpback whale populations recover from past whaling, older males are gaining a major advantage in reproduction. Early in the recovery, breeding groups were dominated by younger whales. But as more mature males returned, they increasingly fathered more calves than their younger rivals. Scientists say experience in singing and competing may help older males win the breeding battle.

environment.title: Ocean temperatures may be protecting Earth from a planet-wide drought
environment.summary: Ocean temperatures may be quietly protecting the world from a global drought catastrophe. By analyzing more than a century of climate data, researchers discovered that droughts rarely spread across the planet at the same time, affecting only about 1.8%–6.5% of global land simultaneously—far less than earlier estimates. The reason lies largely in shifting ocean patterns such as El Niño and La Niña, which create a patchwork of drought conditions across continents instead of one massive worldwide dry spell.

environment.title: T. rex took 40 years to reach full size, study finds
environment.summary: Tyrannosaurus rex may have taken far longer to grow up than scientists once thought. By analyzing growth rings in fossilized leg bones from 17 tyrannosaur specimens and using new statistical methods, researchers found that the famous predator likely took about 40 years to reach its full size—around eight tons—rather than the previously estimated 25 years.

environment.title: Scientists just looked inside Darwin’s 200-year-old specimen jars without opening them
environment.summary: Scientists have used a laser technique to analyze Charles Darwin’s original Galápagos specimens without opening their nearly 200-year-old jars. By shining light through the glass, the method reveals the chemical makeup of the preservation fluids inside. Researchers successfully identified the contents in most samples, offering new clues about historical preservation practices. The breakthrough could help museums protect millions of delicate specimens without risking damage.

environment.title: Scientists discover the protein that malaria parasites can’t live without
environment.summary: Scientists have uncovered a crucial weakness in the malaria parasite that could open the door to new treatments. Researchers identified a protein called Aurora-related kinase 1 (ARK1) that acts like a traffic controller during the parasite’s unusual cell division process, ensuring its genetic material is properly separated as it multiplies. When scientists switched off ARK1 in laboratory experiments, the parasite could no longer replicate correctly and failed to complete its life cycle in both humans and mosquitoes—effectively halting its ability to spread.

environment.title: 2700-year-old teeth reveal the hidden lives of Iron Age Italians
environment.summary: Iron Age teeth from southern Italy have become time capsules, preserving intimate details of childhood and diet. Growth lines in the enamel reveal moments of early-life stress, while hardened plaque holds microscopic remains of cereals, legumes, and fermented foods. The findings suggest a community with diverse food resources and strong Mediterranean connections. Even a small sample offers a striking glimpse into how people lived, grew, and ate nearly three millennia ago.

environment.title: Blasted off Mars and still alive
environment.summary: A famously resilient bacterium may be tough enough to survive one of the most violent events imaginable on Mars. In laboratory experiments designed to mimic the crushing shock of a massive asteroid impact, researchers squeezed Deinococcus radiodurans between steel plates and blasted it with pressures reaching 3 GPa (30,000 times atmospheric pressure). Even under these extreme conditions, a significant portion of the microbes survived.

environment.title: Scoria Cones on Earth and Mars
environment.summary: The hill-shaped features are a sign of explosive volcanic activity—a rarity on the Red Planet. The post Scoria Cones on Earth and Mars appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Chesapeake Bay Locked in Ice
environment.summary: Nearly 50 years ago, the first Landsat satellite captured the rare sight of Mid-Atlantic waterways frozen over. The post Chesapeake Bay Locked in Ice appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.summary: Descoperă beneficiile ieșirilor culturale pentru copii și modul în care muzeele, teatrul și excursiile contribuie la dezvoltarea lor emoțională și educațională. The post Beneficiile ieșirilor culturale în dezvoltarea copiilor appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

mix_items.title: Rodica Bretin lansează romanul puzzle „Pisica de la ora cinci” la Librăria Libris din Brașov
mix_items.summary: Joi, 12 februarie, de la ora 18:00, la Librăria Libris (Str. Șt. O. Iosif) din Brașov, Rodica Bretin lansează volumul „Pisica de la ora cinci”, apărut în colecția Corint Fiction. … Read More The post Rodica Bretin lansează romanul puzzle „Pisica de la ora cinci” la Librăria Libris din Brașov appeared first on Stiri pozitive .

mix_items.title: Sondaj World Vision România: 99% dintre adolescenți folosesc AI, 80.96% au întâlnit conținut fals creat cu AI, iar 37.25% învață despre folosirea sigură a AI din online și social media
mix_items.summary: World Vision România: Avem nevoie de o strategie privind folosirea AI în școală și educație media Ziua Siguranței pe Internet,... Articolul Sondaj World Vision România: 99% dintre adolescenți folosesc AI, 80.96% au întâlnit conținut fals creat cu AI, iar 37.25% învață despre folosirea sigură a AI din online și social media apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Study finds overconfident CEOs are 10-15% less likely to delegate deal work
mix_items.summary: A new study finds overconfident CEOs are less likely to delegate responsibilities to underlings, particularly in settings that involve complex transactions—such as hammering out the details of high-stakes deals. The paper, "Leave it to Me: Overconfident CEOs' Lower Propensity to Delegate Acquisition Responsibility," is published in the Journal of Management Studies.

mix_items.title: What's that critter? New tech guidelines can help ensure we get the right answer
mix_items.summary: Have you ever been in the woods and wondered what bug you're hearing or which bird is singing? These days, new technologies are helping both scientists and the public identify what's trilling, scuttling, tweeting, rustling, or slithering around a given area.

mix_items.title: Snow flies produce bursts of heat and proteins to avoid freezing, new study finds
mix_items.summary: In a new study, Northwestern University scientists explored how snow flies—small, wingless insects that crawl across snow to find mates and lay eggs—survive in freezing cold temperatures. They discovered this snow-dwelling fly uses a surprising combination of strategies: it generates its own body heat like a mammal and produces antifreeze proteins like an Arctic fish.

mix_items.title: Preparing for the next pandemic: Scientists discover a new class of influenza antivirals
mix_items.summary: Researchers from Leiden University, University of York, University of Barcelona/IQTCUB/ICREA and The Francis Crick Institute report a new class of experimental compounds that powerfully block influenza viruses. The findings offer a potential new strategy for treating flu infections and preparing for future pandemics. The study, published in Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, describes a family of molecules known as "sugar aziridines." These compounds are inspired by the widely used flu drug Oseltamivir (Tamiflu), but are designed to act in a fundamentally different way.

mix_items.title: CERN hails delicate test on transporting antimatter as a scientific success
mix_items.summary: Scientists in Geneva took some antiprotons out for a spin—a very delicate one—in a truck, in a never-tried-before test drive that has been deemed a success.

mix_items.title: AI could spot the next financial crisis—but there's a catch
mix_items.summary: What if AI could predict the next financial meltdown? Sounds like a promising idea, yet as new research finds, the devil is in the details.

mix_items.title: NASA Research Proposes Technology to Seek Earth-Like Exoplanets
mix_items.summary: As NASA seeks to understand the mysteries of the universe, the agency is advancing technologies to locate and explore Earth-like planets far beyond our solar system. A key element of this research involves observing reflected light from exoplanets, which can reveal indicators of Earth-like features such as water and oxygen. However, detecting this faint reflected […]

mix_items.title: NASA’s Water-Hunting Tool Will Help Scout Moon’s South Pole
mix_items.summary: NASA is joining international partners to hunt for ice on the Moon in support of future human exploration. The agency is providing a water-detecting instrument, the Neutron Spectrometer System (NSS), to the Lunar Polar Exploration (LUPEX) mission led by JAXA (Japan Aerospace Exploration Agency) and ISRO (Indian Space Research Organisation).

mix_items.title: Teen leaders take on child marriage in Bangladesh
mix_items.summary: Child marriage is a persistent challenge in Bangladesh, but a project to tackle the practice has shown promise The post Teen leaders take on child marriage in Bangladesh appeared first on Positive News .

mix_items.title: Feeling more creative in nature? It might just be a feeling
mix_items.summary: Dr. Amy Lim, from Murdoch University's School of Psychology, and her team examined the effects of nature and non-natural stimuli on creative performance and intentions. The study, titled "Nature versus non-natural environments: Effects on stress and creativity," was published in The Journal of Environmental Psychology.

mix_items.title: As demand for GLP-1 pills and shots surges, healthy habits are still key
mix_items.summary: Whether they're using weekly shots or daily pills, more Americans than ever are turning to anti-obesity drugs to lose weight and boost health.

mix_items.title: UK study finds no added benefit of surfactant treatment for babies with severe bronchiolitis
mix_items.summary: A major UK-led clinical trial has found that a treatment commonly used to help premature babies breathe offers no benefit for infants on life support with severe bronchiolitis—a seasonal viral illness that hospitalizes thousands of babies each year. The Bronchiolitis Endotracheal Surfactant Study (BESS) trial is the largest-ever randomized study of surfactant for bronchiolitis.

mix_items.title: Children's extended social media use linked to increased depression and anxiety
mix_items.summary: Children who use social media for more than three hours per day are more likely to develop greater levels of depression and anxiety compared to those who use it more moderately. The findings are the latest analysis to come from the SCAMP study, led by public health researchers at Imperial College London and based on data from more than 2300 children in schools across London.

mix_items.title: Lyme disease vaccine shows over 70% efficacy, but phase three misses main goal
mix_items.summary: An experimental vaccine for Lyme disease is broadly effective, pharmaceutical companies Pfizer and Valneva announced Monday; however, the latter firm's shares fell because the clinical trial did not reach its main goal.

mix_items.title: FDA approves higher-dose Wegovy to help people lose more weight
mix_items.summary: A stronger version of the popular weight loss drug Wegovy is on the way after federal regulators signed off on a higher dose. The U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved a 7.2-milligram dose of Novo Nordisk's semaglutide, the active ingredient in Wegovy. Until now, the highest approved dose was 2.4 milligrams, taken as a weekly shot.

mix_items.title: Fathers' risk for psychiatric disorders increases a year after child's birth, study finds
mix_items.summary: Fathers in Sweden are less likely to receive a psychiatric diagnosis during their partner's pregnancy and in the months following the birth of their child. However, diagnoses of depression and stress-related disorders increase a year later, according to a new study published in JAMA Network Open by researchers at Karolinska Institutet in Sweden and Sichuan University in China.

mix_items.title: The prisoners flipping the script on stage
mix_items.summary: A theatre project brings prisoners to the stage, challenging how society sees them – and how they see themselves The post The prisoners flipping the script on stage appeared first on Positive News .

mix_items.title: Study finds two types of colon polyps can raise bowel cancer risk fivefold
mix_items.summary: Researchers studying over 8,400 colonoscopies discovered that having both adenomas and serrated polyps in the bowel can raise the risk of serious precancerous changes by up to five times. These two polyp types may represent separate cancer pathways that can occur at the same time. Nearly half of patients with serrated polyps also had adenomas, making this high-risk combination more common than expected. The results emphasize the importance of early detection and regular colonoscopy monitoring.

mix_items.title: Severe COVID or flu may raise lung cancer risk years later
mix_items.summary: A severe case of COVID-19 or influenza could increase the risk of lung cancer later on, according to new research. Scientists discovered that serious viral infections can alter immune cells in the lungs, leaving behind chronic inflammation that may help tumors develop months or years later. The increased risk was seen mainly after severe infections that required hospitalization. Vaccination, however, appears to prevent the dangerous lung changes.

mix_items.title: Monty Python Got It Wrong About Medieval Disease
mix_items.summary: In medieval Denmark, people could pay for more prestigious graves closer to the church — a sign of wealth and status. But when researchers examined hundreds of skeletons, they discovered something unexpected: even people with stigmatized diseases like leprosy were buried in these high-status spots. Instead of excluding the sick, many communities appear to have treated them much like everyone else.

mix_items.title: Humpback whale recovery is changing who fathers the calves
mix_items.summary: A new study shows that as humpback whale populations recover from past whaling, older males are gaining a major advantage in reproduction. Early in the recovery, breeding groups were dominated by younger whales. But as more mature males returned, they increasingly fathered more calves than their younger rivals. Scientists say experience in singing and competing may help older males win the breeding battle.

mix_items.title: Ocean temperatures may be protecting Earth from a planet-wide drought
mix_items.summary: Ocean temperatures may be quietly protecting the world from a global drought catastrophe. By analyzing more than a century of climate data, researchers discovered that droughts rarely spread across the planet at the same time, affecting only about 1.8%–6.5% of global land simultaneously—far less than earlier estimates. The reason lies largely in shifting ocean patterns such as El Niño and La Niña, which create a patchwork of drought conditions across continents instead of one massive worldwide dry spell.

mix_items.title: T. rex took 40 years to reach full size, study finds
mix_items.summary: Tyrannosaurus rex may have taken far longer to grow up than scientists once thought. By analyzing growth rings in fossilized leg bones from 17 tyrannosaur specimens and using new statistical methods, researchers found that the famous predator likely took about 40 years to reach its full size—around eight tons—rather than the previously estimated 25 years.

mix_items.title: Scientists just looked inside Darwin’s 200-year-old specimen jars without opening them
mix_items.summary: Scientists have used a laser technique to analyze Charles Darwin’s original Galápagos specimens without opening their nearly 200-year-old jars. By shining light through the glass, the method reveals the chemical makeup of the preservation fluids inside. Researchers successfully identified the contents in most samples, offering new clues about historical preservation practices. The breakthrough could help museums protect millions of delicate specimens without risking damage.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover the protein that malaria parasites can’t live without
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered a crucial weakness in the malaria parasite that could open the door to new treatments. Researchers identified a protein called Aurora-related kinase 1 (ARK1) that acts like a traffic controller during the parasite’s unusual cell division process, ensuring its genetic material is properly separated as it multiplies. When scientists switched off ARK1 in laboratory experiments, the parasite could no longer replicate correctly and failed to complete its life cycle in both humans and mosquitoes—effectively halting its ability to spread.

mix_items.title: 2700-year-old teeth reveal the hidden lives of Iron Age Italians
mix_items.summary: Iron Age teeth from southern Italy have become time capsules, preserving intimate details of childhood and diet. Growth lines in the enamel reveal moments of early-life stress, while hardened plaque holds microscopic remains of cereals, legumes, and fermented foods. The findings suggest a community with diverse food resources and strong Mediterranean connections. Even a small sample offers a striking glimpse into how people lived, grew, and ate nearly three millennia ago.

mix_items.title: Blasted off Mars and still alive
mix_items.summary: A famously resilient bacterium may be tough enough to survive one of the most violent events imaginable on Mars. In laboratory experiments designed to mimic the crushing shock of a massive asteroid impact, researchers squeezed Deinococcus radiodurans between steel plates and blasted it with pressures reaching 3 GPa (30,000 times atmospheric pressure). Even under these extreme conditions, a significant portion of the microbes survived.

mix_items.title: Scoria Cones on Earth and Mars
mix_items.summary: The hill-shaped features are a sign of explosive volcanic activity—a rarity on the Red Planet. The post Scoria Cones on Earth and Mars appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Chesapeake Bay Locked in Ice
mix_items.summary: Nearly 50 years ago, the first Landsat satellite captured the rare sight of Mid-Atlantic waterways frozen over. The post Chesapeake Bay Locked in Ice appeared first on NASA Science .

joke_ro.text: Mi-am facut lista de treburi. Acum am doua: lista si vinovatia.

satire_ro.title: Auzind că Luca a ieftinit covrigii de la 2,5 la 2 lei, Bolojan anunţă o taxă de 50 de bani pe covrig
